---
title: "Chapter 2 Notes"
author: "John Lockwood"
format: html
editor: visual
---

These are notes are based on *Discovering Statistics Using R*, part of my [Statistics 1](../DiscoveringStatisticsUsingR/Statistics_01.qmd) course.\

## Chapter 2: Everything you ever wanted to know about statistics (well, sort of)

Begun 7/7/2026

Really the chapter is about statistical models. We build these for the same reason engineers build models of bridges — to try to understand how it will perform in the real world. To make sure the model represents the world accurately, need a good model **fit** to the real world data.

In psychology, analysis of variance (ANOVA) and regression are both **linear models.** I.e., try to fit a straight line. Curves are non-linear models. The book deals only with linear models (I'm starting to think it's pretty basic).

### Populations and Samples

We try to make predictions about broad populations (e.g. all humans), but by necessity, our samples are smaller. Bigger samples are better (up to a point, presumably).

Can measure fit of a model as difference between line we draw to represent data and actual points, as:

- **Total error.** This is the sum of all the deviances. But this bad because positive and negative will cancel. Son instead we use

- **Sum of squares** - Better, now always dealing with positive numbers. But varies depending on \# of data points, so instead use:

- **Mean Sum of Squares** (aka Mean Square): Take sum of squares / \# of data points -1 (Degrees of freedom, or $df$ ). Degrees of freedom is like putting eggs in carton. At first you can put egg anywhere in 18 slots. Then for next you can only choose 17, etc. Last egg you have no choice, only one slot left. So for an 18 egg crate, have 18 - 1 = 17 $df$. This is known as the ***variance***.

- **Standard deviation.** Take the variance, which is based on mean squares, and take the square root to adjust for that, i.e.

$$s = \sqrt{\frac{\sum~(x_1 - \bar{x})^2}{N - 1}}$$

Standard deviation is not just about the fit of our data, a small SD means a narrower distribution, a large SD means a wider one.\
\
Thanks to [Gemini](https://share.gemini.google/h9Jk6tqmz3OD)for some sample code showing how this works. The trick is making sure xlim is set, otherwise autos-scaling means the graph always appears to be the same width.

In effect, a small SD means all values cluster around the mean tightly (good fit), where a large SD means we have a "bad" fit, since the values are more spread out w/ respect to the mean.

```{r}
# 1. Generate two datasets with the same mean but different standard deviations
data_narrow <- rnorm(1000, mean = 50, sd = 3)
data_wide <- rnorm(1000, mean = 50, sd = 15)

# 2. Plot them side-by-side (sets up a 1 row, 2 column layout)
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))

# 3. Plot both using the SAME xlim (e.g., 0 to 100)
hist(
  data_narrow, 
  xlim = c(0, 100),            # Locks the axis
  breaks = 20,
  col = "steelblue", 
  main = "Narrow (SD = 3)"
)

hist(
  data_wide, 
  xlim = c(0, 100),            # Locks the axis to match
  breaks = 20,
  col = "coral", 
  main = "Wide (SD = 15)"
)

# Reset the plot layout back to normal when done
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```

The mean is also a model, because all equations in stats boil down to:

$$\text{outcome}_i = \text{(model)} +\text{error}_i$$

For standard deviation, we have:

$$deviation = \sum{(observed - model)}^2$$

So far we've dealt with fit of our model to sample, but what we really want to know is fit of model to the whole population. For this we introduce the **standard error**.

For example, using mean, we can say perhaps that we take 9 samples of lecturers popularity, and get a value of 2 for one sample, 3 for another, etc. as a mean. This is **sampling variation**, and we can plot this as a histogram too. This is a sampling distribution.

p\. 43, "The standard deviation of sample means is known as the std error of the mean (SE)."

But we can't really collect hundreds of samples, so we use the **central limit theorem** (and we're sneaking up on confidence intervals in this chapter too)...

The idea that as sample gets large, i.e. \> 30, standard deviation of the sampling distribution converges to a std normal distribution is given by:

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{s}{\sqrt{N}}$$

When we talked about Z scores (mean of 0 and SD of 1), we said that 95% of z scores will fall between a value of -1.96 and 1.96. For a 99% confidence interval, we would use the values between which 99% of the scores fall, -2.58 and 2.58.\
\
He gives a way to solve for any given confidence interval, by looking it up in the for the std normal distribution. [This post](https://www.r-bloggers.com/2021/11/calculate-confidence-intervals-in-r/) shows how you would solve it in R. I'm still not sure I understand it well, though.
